content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The train leaves ___ 7 o'clock every morning.
distractor_rationale: in is used for months, years, and periods, not clock times
on is used for days and dates, not clock times
by indicates a deadline, not the scheduled time of a departure

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My birthday is ___ March, so we always have a party then.
distractor_rationale: at is used for clock times and fixed expressions like at night, not months
on is used for specific days and dates, not months
during requires a noun that describes an event or period, not a bare month name used to state when something occurs

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The supermarket is closed ___ Sunday, so we need to shop on Saturday.
distractor_rationale: in is used for months, years, and seasons, not days of the week
at is used for clock times and fixed phrases, not days of the week
during is used before event nouns such as the weekend or the holidays, not before a single named day

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom learned to drive ___ the summer holidays when he was eighteen.
distractor_rationale: in the summer holidays is unnatural because the summer holidays is a specific event period, not a general season or year; during is required before definite event-period nouns
at is used for clock times and brief fixed moments, not extended event periods
on is used for specific days and dates, not extended holiday periods

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Please send me the report ___ Friday — the manager needs it before the weekend meeting.
distractor_rationale: until describes a continuous state or action lasting up to a point, not a one-time submission deadline
at is used for clock times, not deadline days
on Friday alone would mean the action happens on that day but does not convey the sense of a deadline before which the report must arrive

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The children stayed in the playground ___ half past three, when their teacher called them inside.
distractor_rationale: by indicates a deadline for completing an action, not a continuous activity that continues up to a point in time
during is followed by a noun describing an event or period, not a specific clock time
at marks a point in time when something happens, not the end point of a continuous activity

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The new sports centre is open ___ Monday ___ Saturday, but it is closed on Sundays.
distractor_rationale: during is not used to introduce the start of a range, and until alone after the second day is non-standard in this fixed range pattern
between requires and, not at, and at is for clock times not days
from … until can express duration but is less natural and standard than from … to when listing two days as the limits of a weekly schedule

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna first moved to the city ___ 2018, and she has lived there ever since.
distractor_rationale: at is used for clock times and fixed phrases such as at night, not for years
on is used for specific days and full calendar dates, not for years
during is used before event-period nouns, not before a bare year number

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The builders must finish all the repairs ___ the end of next month, or the company will pay a fine.
distractor_rationale: until implies a continuous activity or state lasting up to a point, but finishing repairs is a one-time completed action with a deadline
at is used for clock times and brief moments, not for month-end deadlines
during is followed by an event-period noun and cannot introduce a deadline for a completed action

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The hotel receptionist told us that the pool would be closed ___ the renovation work, which was expected to last two weeks.
distractor_rationale: while introduces a clause with a subject and verb, but the renovation work is a noun phrase, not a clause, so during is required
for indicates a length of time measured in units and would require a number such as for two weeks, not for the renovation work
until marks the end point of a continuous state, not the event period within which something is closed

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The supermarket opens ___ 8 o'clock every morning.
distractor_rationale: in is used for months, years, and periods, not clock times
on is used for days and dates, not clock times
during requires a noun phrase describing a period, not a specific clock time

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom has football practice ___ Saturdays.
distractor_rationale: at is used for clock times, not days of the week
in is used for months, years, and seasons, not days of the week
by expresses a deadline, not a recurring day

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna fell asleep ___ the long bus journey and missed her stop.
distractor_rationale: in does not collocate with a specific event noun like 'the long bus journey' to mean 'throughout that event'
at is reserved for clock times and specific moments, not extended events
on is used for days and dates, not for describing what happened throughout an event

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Please send me the completed report ___ Friday, because the manager needs it for the weekend meeting.
distractor_rationale: until describes a continuous state or action lasting up to a point, not a deadline for submitting something
at is used with clock times, not with day names expressing a deadline
in is used with months, years, or seasons, not with day names expressing a deadline

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The café stays open ___ midnight on weekends, so we can meet there after the show.
distractor_rationale: by indicates a deadline for completing an action, not a continuous state lasting up to a point in time
from cannot stand alone here; it requires a paired 'to' or 'until' to form a range, and using 'from midnight' would reverse the meaning
during requires a noun phrase describing an event or period, not a specific clock time like midnight

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: My birthday is ___ March, so we usually have a party then.
distractor_rationale: 'at' is used for clock times and specific points, not months
'on' is used for specific days and dates, not months
'by' expresses a deadline, not the time of year when something occurs

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara always visits her grandmother ___ Sundays.
distractor_rationale: 'in' is used for months and years, not days of the week
'at' is used for clock times and fixed expressions like 'at the weekend' in British English, but not with named days
'during' requires a noun phrase describing a period or event, not a day name

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The children were very noisy ___ the film, so we could not hear the dialogue clearly.
distractor_rationale: 'while' is a conjunction and must be followed by a clause with a subject and verb, not a noun phrase
'at' is used for clock times and specific points, not for describing an ongoing event period
'in' is used for months, years, and seasons, not for describing a concurrent event

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The library is open ___ Monday to Friday, but it closes at the weekend.
distractor_rationale: 'at' is used for clock times and specific points, not to introduce the start of a range
'during' is used before a noun phrase describing a period, not to connect two endpoints of a range
'by' expresses a deadline, not the beginning of a time span

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom started learning to drive ___ 2019, and he passed his test the following year.
distractor_rationale: 'at' is used for clock times and fixed time expressions, not years
'on' is used for specific days and dates, not years
'during' is used before a noun phrase describing an event or period, not a bare year

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Please make sure you submit your application ___ Friday, or the manager will not consider it.
distractor_rationale: 'until' describes a continuous state or action that continues up to a point, not a one-time deadline for completion
'at' is used for clock times, not for expressing a submission deadline on a named day
'during' requires a noun phrase describing a period or event, not a specific day acting as a deadline

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The café stays open ___ midnight every night, so you can always get a hot drink late.
distractor_rationale: 'by' expresses a deadline for completing an action, not a continuous state that lasts up to a point in time
'at' is used for clock times as a point of reference, not to describe the end point of a continuous duration
'from' introduces the start of a time range and must be paired with 'to' or 'until', so it cannot stand alone here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna had already finished her report ___ the time the meeting started, so she felt well prepared.
distractor_rationale: 'until' describes a continuous situation lasting up to a point, but finishing a report is a completed action, not a continuous one — 'by' marks the deadline before which it was done
'during' is followed by a noun phrase describing an event, not by the expression 'the time' introducing a clause
'at' can introduce a time expression but 'at the time' means 'at that moment in the past', which does not convey the sense of prior completion that the context requires

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The sports centre is available for bookings ___ 6 a.m. to 10 p.m. on weekdays.
distractor_rationale: 'during' is used before a noun phrase describing a period or event, not to introduce the start point of a clock-time range with 'to'
'between' pairs with 'and' in time ranges (between 6 a.m. and 10 p.m.), not with 'to', so it creates an ungrammatical structure here
'at' is used for a single specific clock time, not to introduce the beginning of a time span

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The class starts in 9 o'clock every morning.
content.error_sentence: The class starts in 9 o'clock every morning.
content.corrected_sentence: The class starts at 9 o'clock every morning.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: My birthday is in Monday this year.
content.error_sentence: My birthday is in Monday this year.
content.corrected_sentence: My birthday is on Monday this year.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom fell asleep during the film was playing in the living room.
content.error_sentence: Tom fell asleep during the film was playing in the living room.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom fell asleep while the film was playing in the living room.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Please finish your homework until Friday or you will lose marks.
content.error_sentence: Please finish your homework until Friday or you will lose marks.
content.corrected_sentence: Please finish your homework by Friday or you will lose marks.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna usually goes for a walk at the afternoon when the weather is nice.
content.error_sentence: Anna usually goes for a walk at the afternoon when the weather is nice.
content.corrected_sentence: Anna usually goes for a walk in the afternoon when the weather is nice.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The shop was open from Monday until Saturday, but it was closed all day on Sunday.
content.error_sentence: The shop was open from Monday until Saturday, but it was closed all day on Sunday.
content.corrected_sentence: The shop was open from Monday to Saturday, but it was closed all day on Sunday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The road works started in 14 March and caused long traffic delays for several weeks.
content.error_sentence: The road works started in 14 March and caused long traffic delays for several weeks.
content.corrected_sentence: The road works started on 14 March and caused long traffic delays for several weeks.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager said we could stay in the meeting room by they finished setting up the equipment.
content.error_sentence: The manager said we could stay in the meeting room by they finished setting up the equipment.
content.corrected_sentence: The manager said we could stay in the meeting room until they finished setting up the equipment.

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The train leaves ___ 7 o'clock every morning.
distractor_rationale: 'in' is used for months, years, and periods, not clock times
'on' is used for days and dates, not clock times
'during' requires a noun phrase describing a period, not a clock time

duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item

rejected.rejection_reason: duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item
